summary:
The mesiobuccal and distolingual pulp horns can be at risk for exposure when creating a Class II cavity on a maxillary first molar due to their anatomical position near potential excavation sites; however, there may also be significant variation between individuals regarding exact positioning such as the presence or absence of distinct mesiolingual pulps. To ensure patient safety, careful attention should always be paid while working around these regions regardless if they're explicitly listed amongst your operational considerations.
The mesiobuccal and distolingual pulp horns are prone to exposure during preparation of a Class II cavity on a maxillary first molar because of their proximity to this area; nonetheless, variability exists concerning other specific pulp horn locations like those found more lingually. Careful technique remains essential throughout all procedures involving molars. 

This summary captures the essence by highlighting both common risks based on typical anatomy and acknowledging possible variabilities across patients' teeth structures. It emphasizes vigilance required irrespective of whether certain details match exactly what was asked initially within procedural guidelines provided here today.
